1.1: Joel is undated — no contemporary king is mentioned — but it is typically placed in the post-exilic period (late 5th-early 4th century BCE) based on its references to the temple cult and its post-exilic vocabulary. The book's structure: a devastating locust plague (chs. 1-2:11) serves as the occasion for a call to repentance (2:12-17), followed by YHWH's promise of restoration (2:18-3:21). The locust plague is both a literal agricultural disaster and the harbinger of the Day of the LORD — the pattern of divine judgment working through historical events that will culminate in the final eschatological judgment. Joel is the OT prophet most cited in the NT on the Day of Pentecost, making his prophecy of the Spirit's universal outpouring the bridge between the Old and New covenant eras.
1.2: 'Has such a thing happened in your days, or in the days of your fathers?' The call to generational memory is a rhetorical device establishing the unprecedented scale of the locust disaster. Agricultural memory in the ancient Near East was long — oral traditions preserved accounts of famines and plagues across generations. The comparison 'tell your children' invokes the Passover-instruction pattern (Exod 12:26-27, 13:8), repurposing the command to transmit salvation-history for transmitting judgment-history.
1.3: 'Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children, and their children to another generation.' The generational transmission formula — this plague is to be remembered alongside the Exodus and other covenant-defining events. The three-generation chain (your children → their children → another generation) parallels the way Deuteronomy instructs covenant memory (Deut 4:9-10, 6:2). A locust plague severe enough to be preserved in generational memory was genuinely catastrophic — modern locust swarms in the Middle East have stripped regions of all vegetation within hours.
1.4: 'What the cutting locust left, the swarming locust has eaten. What the swarming locust left, the hopping locust has eaten, and what the hopping locust left, the destroying locust has eaten.' The four locust terms — gazam (cutting/gnawing), arbeh (swarming, the common Hebrew word for locust), yeleq (hopping/young locust), chasil (destroying/finishing) — have been variously interpreted as four distinct species, four stages of locust development, or a poetic series emphasizing total destruction. Modern entomologists recognize that desert locust swarms (Schistocerca gregaria) can contain 40-80 million insects per square kilometer, stripping 100,000 tons of vegetation per day.
1.5: 'Awake, you drunkards, and weep, and wail, all you drinkers of wine, because of the sweet wine, for it is cut off from your mouth.' The drunkards are addressed first because the wine harvest is the most visible casualty of the locust plague — the grape vines stripped bare. The 'sweet wine' (asis, new wine, grape juice) represents the entire harvest cycle: if the vines are destroyed, there will be no wine that season. Addressing drunkards is also a rhetorical device: even those whose primary interest is self-indulgence should be awakened by this disaster.
1.6: 'For a nation has come up against my land, powerful and beyond number; its teeth are like lions' teeth, and it has the fangs of a lioness.' The locusts are metaphorized as an invading nation (goy) — a military force without number. The lion-teeth image precisely describes locust mandibles: locusts have serrated, V-shaped mandibles capable of biting through dry stalks rapidly. The comparison to lion and lioness teeth (shineihu shinei aryeh veme'talot laviy lo) is also in Prov 30:14, applied to the wicked. The personification of the locust swarm as a foreign army with predatory jaws sets up ch2's extended military metaphor.
1.7: 'It has laid waste my vine and splintered my fig tree; it has stripped off their bark and thrown it down; their branches are made white.' The vine and fig tree were the two most economically and symbolically important agricultural products of ancient Palestine — a standard symbol for peace and prosperity (Mic 4:4: 'every man under his vine and under his fig tree'). The 'white branches' (sarugeiha hivinu) — the peeled, whitened wood after the bark is stripped — is a precisely observed detail of locust damage; the skeletal white branches are the final visible result of total stripping.
1.8: 'Lament like a virgin wearing sackcloth for the bridegroom of her youth.' The mourning image: a young woman lamenting the death of her betrothed before the marriage was consummated. In the ancient Levant, betrothal carried strong legal and emotional weight — the betrothed couple was considered 'husband and wife' legally (Deut 22:23-24) even before the wedding. The mourning posture — wearing sackcloth — is the standard grief garment of the OT (Gen 37:34, 2 Sam 3:31, Isa 22:12). The intensity of this grief is the measure of the community's appropriate response to the disaster.
1.9: 'The grain offering and the drink offering are cut off from the house of the LORD; the priests mourn, the ministers of the LORD.' The daily tamid offering (the perpetual sacrifice, Exod 29:38-42, Num 28:3-8) required grain and wine: two lambs per day, with grain and oil, plus wine. When the locust plague destroyed the crops, the material basis for temple worship was removed. The cessation of the tamid was considered one of the gravest possible covenant crises — Daniel 9:27, 11:31, 12:11 use its 'removal' as a sign of ultimate desolation, and 1 Macc 1:45 records Antiochus IV forbidding it as the defining act of desecration.
1.10: 'The fields are destroyed, the ground mourns, because the grain is destroyed, the wine dries up, the oil languishes.' The three staple crops of Palestinian agriculture: grain (dagan), wine (tirosh), and oil (yitzhar) — the triad that represents covenant blessing in Deuteronomy (7:13, 11:14, 12:17). The 'mourning' of the ground (avela ha-adamah) is a personification — land mourning appears in Hos 4:3 and Jer 12:4, 23:10. Ancient Israelites understood the land as a covenant participant: when the covenant is broken, the land itself is affected (Lev 26:20, 32-35).
1.11: 'Be ashamed, O tillers of the soil; wail, O vinedressers, for the wheat and the barley, because the harvest of the field has perished.' The shame (hobbishu) of farmers is the shame of skilled workers whose craft has become futile — the land has rejected their labor. Wheat (chitah) and barley (se'orah) were the two main grain crops: wheat for bread, barley for poorer bread and animal feed. The 'harvest of the field' (qetzir sadeh) represents the entire year's agricultural output — not just a single crop.
1.12: 'The vine dries up; the fig tree languishes. Pomegranate, palm, and apple — all the trees of the field are dried up, and gladness dries up from the children of man.' The enumeration of fruit trees — vine (gefen), fig (te'enah), pomegranate (rimon), date palm (tamar), apple (tappuach) — is a catalog of Palestinian horticulture. These slow-growing trees represent multi-generational investment; their destruction is catastrophic beyond a single season. 'Gladness dries up from the children of man' (yavesh sos min-benei adam) — joy is agricultural in the ancient world; when the harvest fails, the social fabric of celebration collapses.
1.13: 'Put on sackcloth and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar. Go in, pass the night in sackcloth, O ministers of my God, because the grain offering and the drink offering are withheld from the house of your God.' The priests are given a specific instruction: 'lie in sackcloth all night' (linu basaqim) — overnight lament before YHWH, a form of extended mourning vigil. The dual address 'my God... your God' moves between divine and human perspective: YHWH calls the priests 'ministers of my God' (YHWH speaking), then they are instructed about 'the house of your God' — the same temple from both perspectives.
1.14: 'Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly. Gather the elders and all the inhabitants of the land to the house of the LORD your God, and cry out to the LORD.' The national covenant ceremony (miqra qodesh, solemn assembly) was the standard community crisis response in ancient Israel — cf. 2 Chr 20:3-4 (Jehoshaphat's crisis fast), Ezra 8:21-23 (Ezra's fast before returning from Babylon). The gathering of 'elders and all inhabitants' parallels Deut 31:12: 'assemble the people, men, women, and little ones, and the sojourner within your towns.' The solemn assembly activates the covenant machinery of corporate petition.
1.15: 'Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and as destruction from the Almighty it comes.' First mention of the key theological concept: the Day of YHWH (yom YHWH). The concept appears in Amos 5:18-20, Isa 2:12, Zeph 1:14-18, and Ezek 7:10 — a day when YHWH intervenes in history with decisive judgment. The wordplay on 'Almighty' is characteristic: 'destruction from the Almighty' (ushod mishadday yavo) — shod (destruction) echoes Shadday (Almighty, El Shaddai). Amos (5:18-20) had warned that the Day of YHWH would be darkness, not light, for Israel — Joel develops this warning with the locust as its embodiment.
1.16: 'Is not the food cut off before our eyes, joy and gladness from the house of our God?' The rhetorical question makes visible the connection between material loss and spiritual crisis. 'Before our eyes' (le-neinu min-beyt Eloheinu) — the loss is visible, undeniable. The 'house of our God' is the temple, the center of national worship and celebration. The three pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles) were occasions of communal joy (simchah); when the agricultural basis for those celebrations is destroyed, the joy is 'cut off' (nigzar) as surely as the crops.
1.17: 'The seed shrivels under the clods; the storehouses are desolate; the granaries are torn down because the grain has dried up.' Specific agricultural detail: seed planted in the ground (parot tachath megrephotam) — the seeds have dried up under their clods, failing to germinate or withered before germination. The 'storehouses' (mammegarot) and 'granaries' (asamim) torn down because they have nothing to protect. Ancient storage pits and granary buildings were large community infrastructure investments; emptying and abandoning them represents the total collapse of the agricultural system.
1.18: 'How the beasts groan! The herds of cattle are perplexed because there is no pasture for them; even the flocks of sheep suffer.' The suffering of animals during a locust plague is ecologically precise: when locusts strip the vegetation, domestic animals have no pasture. The 'perplexity' (nashemu, desolated/confused) of cattle is a strong verb — large animals that normally exhibit calm purposefulness are disoriented. Ancient Israelite agriculture was mixed: grain, horticulture, and herding were integrated, so the loss of pasture compounds the loss of grain.
1.19: 'To you, O LORD, I call. For fire has devoured the pastures of the wilderness, and flame has burned all the trees of the field.' The prophet's prayer begins here — shifting from description (vv1-18) to petition. 'Fire' (esh) devouring the pastures may be literal (fires that sometimes accompany or follow locust invasions when dry vegetation burns) or metaphorical (the locusts' effect visually resembling scorched earth). The 'pastures of the wilderness' (ne'ot midbar) are the uncultivated grazing lands — even the areas too marginal for agriculture are stripped.
1.20: 'Even the beasts of the field pant for you because the water brooks are dried up, and fire has devoured the pastures of the wilderness.' The wild animals join the community of petition — they 'pant for' YHWH (ta'arog eleykha) as Ps 42:1 uses the same word for the deer panting for water. The drying of the water brooks (aphikey mayim) alongside the locust damage represents a compound disaster: locust plague and drought together. The inclusion of wild animals in the prayer reflects the OT's view of all creation as within YHWH's covenant care (cf. Ps 36:6, Jonah 4:11).

2.1: 'Blow a trumpet in Zion; sound an alarm on my holy mountain! Let all the inhabitants of the land tremble, for the day of the LORD is coming; it is near.' The shofar blown in Zion is the war alarm — the signal to gather the community for defense (cf. Num 10:9, Amos 3:6). The 'holy mountain' is Jerusalem/Zion itself (cf. Ps 48:1). The second 'the day of the LORD is near' (first in 1:15) — the locust invasion is being reinterpreted: it is not merely an agricultural disaster but a harbinger of the ultimate Day. The connection between local crisis and cosmic eschatology is characteristic of Joel's theological method.
2.2: 'A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness! Like blackness there is spread upon the mountains a great and powerful people; their like has never been before, nor will be again after them through the years of all generations.' 'Darkness and gloom, clouds and thick darkness' (yom choshek va'afelah yom anan va'araphel) are the classic theophany terms: Sinai (Exod 19:16, 20:21), the Day of YHWH in Zeph 1:15 ('a day of darkness and gloom'), and the NT's description of the crucifixion darkness (Matt 27:45). The 'great and powerful people' is the locust army. 'Their like has never been before' — the same formula as the Egyptian plagues (Exod 10:14: 'there had never been such locusts').
2.3: 'Fire devours before them, and behind them a flame burns. The land is like the garden of Eden before them, but behind them a desolate wilderness, and nothing escapes them.' Before the locust swarm: a garden. Behind them: wilderness. This is an exact description of locust devastation — aerial photographs of locust swarms show the precise boundary between stripped and unstripped vegetation. 'The garden of Eden' before, 'a desolate wilderness' behind — the reversal of creation's abundance to pre-creation chaos. The Eden reference establishes that this is not just agricultural loss but a cosmological reversal.
2.4: 'Their appearance is like the appearance of horses, and like war horses they run.' The comparison of locusts to horses is cross-cultural: the Italian word for locust, cavalletta (little horse), reflects the same visual observation. The locust's head actually resembles a small horse — this is so consistent across cultures that it is preserved in Rev 9:7 ('the locusts looked like horses prepared for battle'). The 'war horse' image introduces the military metaphor that dominates vv4-9, making the agricultural disaster into a cosmic military campaign.
2.5: 'As with the rumbling of chariots, they leap on the tops of the mountains, like the crackling of a flame of fire devouring the stubble, like a powerful army drawn up for battle.' Three comparisons: (1) chariots rumbling — the sound of a locust swarm is indeed like thunder or distant wheels; (2) fire crackling over stubble — the dry crackle of locust movement through grain; (3) an army in battle array (am atzum meurak milchamah). The triple comparison builds the military metaphor: sound, appearance, and organization all correspond to an army. Ancient armies and locusts were both dreaded phenomena beyond individual control.
2.6: 'Before them peoples are in anguish; all faces grow pale.' The fear response to the locust army: peoples in anguish (yachilu, writhe in birth-pangs) and faces 'gathering blackness' (kol-panim qibetzu fharur) — the Hebrew idiom for the pallor of terror. The same face-blackening appears in Nah 2:10 (Nineveh's fall) — a physiological description of fear-induced pallor. The scope has expanded: it is no longer just Israel that trembles before this army, but 'peoples' (ammim) — the locust plague/Day of YHWH has universal scope.
2.7: 'They run like mighty warriors; they climb the wall like soldiers; they march each on his way; they do not swerve from their paths.' The disciplined military advance of the locust swarm is a well-observed phenomenon: locust swarms do not deviate from their direction of advance; individual locusts maintain their course even while climbing vertical surfaces. 'They do not swerve from their paths' (velo ye'abetun oruchotam) — the same verb (avar) used of armies that do not deviate from their assigned lines. ANE military precision was the ideal; the locusts surpass even that ideal.
2.8: 'They do not jostle one another; each marches in his path; they burst through the weapons and are not halted.' Within the swarm, individual locusts do not interfere with each other — they move through narrow spaces without breaking formation. 'They burst through the weapons' (uvaad hashelach yipolu velo yivtzeru) — even weapons cannot stop them. This is the locust plague's terrifying quality from a military perspective: there is no defense against it. The locust army is invincible — which is the theological point: this is YHWH's army (v11), and no earthly defense can resist YHWH's judgment.
2.9: 'They leap upon the city, they run upon the walls, they climb up into the houses, they enter through the windows like a thief.' The total penetration of the city: locusts climb walls, enter buildings through any opening. Contemporaneous accounts of locust invasions describe exactly this behavior — locusts entering houses, filling rooms, impossible to exclude. The 'thief through the window' image appears in Jer 9:21 for death entering through windows. The city that is supposed to provide security against enemies provides none against this divinely-sent army.
2.10: 'The earth quakes before them; the heavens tremble. The sun and the moon are darkened, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The cosmic response to the locust army — the sun darkened as a large swarm passes (historically observed; a dense swarm can reduce sunlight noticeably), but now expanded to cosmic theophany. 'Earth quakes, heavens tremble' are standard theophanic language (Ps 68:8, Judg 5:4-5, Hab 3:6). The darkening of sun, moon, and stars will recur in Joel 3:15 and is the signature cosmic sign of the Day of YHWH — cited in Matt 24:29, Mark 13:24-25, and Rev 6:12.
2.11: 'The LORD utters his voice before his army, for his camp is exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful. For the day of the LORD is great and very awesome; who can endure it?' YHWH 'utters his voice' (YHWH natan qolo) — thunder before his army, as in Ps 29 (the thunder-theophany) and Ps 46:6. The army of locusts is explicitly identified as YHWH's army (machaneihu, his camp). The rhetorical question 'who can endure it?' (mi yechilenu) — who can sustain the weight of the Day of YHWH? — finds its NT echo in Rev 6:17: 'the great day of their wrath has come, and who can stand?'
2.12: 'Yet even now, declares the LORD, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning.' The divine 'yet even now' (vegam-attah) — even in the midst of judgment, even at this late hour, repentance remains possible. This is the defining characteristic of Hosea's and Joel's prophetic theology: judgment is not absolute fate but a call to return. 'With all your heart' echoes Deut 6:5 (the Shema) and Deut 30:10 (the call to return). The three outward expressions of repentance — fasting (tzom), weeping (bechi), mourning (misped) — are the physical forms of the internal turn.
2.13: 'And rend your hearts and not your garments. Return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love; and he relents over disaster.' The call to 'rend your hearts and not your garments' is one of the OT's most precise distinctions between external religious form and internal reality. Tearing garments was the standard mourning gesture (Gen 37:34, 2 Sam 1:11, 3:31, Ezra 9:3); Joel demands the tearing be internal. The divine character description — 'gracious and merciful, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love' — is the Exodus 34:6-7 formula (YHWH's own self-description to Moses), foundational to Israel's covenant theology and cited in Jonah 4:2, Ps 86:15, 103:8, 145:8, Neh 9:17.
2.14: 'Who knows whether he will not turn and relent, and leave a blessing behind him, a grain offering and a drink offering for the LORD your God?' The 'who knows' (mi yodea) is the language of humble uncertainty about divine response — the same phrase used by the king of Nineveh in Jonah 3:9: 'who knows whether God will not turn and relent?' This parallel between Joel 2:14 and Jonah 3:9 suggests either contemporaneity or literary dependence. The 'blessing' left behind is specifically agricultural — grain offering and drink offering, the same offerings cut off in 1:9. Repentance may restore what the locust took.
2.15: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.' A second shofar-blast in Zion — the first (v1) was for alarm; this one is for assembly. The sequence mirrors the Mosaic instructions for trumpet-blowing in Num 10:1-10: one blast for assembly, two for alarm. The solemn assembly (atzarah) was the technical term for the closing assembly of major festivals (Lev 23:36, Num 29:35, Deut 16:8) — now repurposed for emergency fasting. The progression from alarm (v1) to assembly (v15) marks the movement from crisis to response.
2.16: 'Gather the people. Consecrate the congregation; assemble the elders; gather the children, even nursing infants. Let the bridegroom leave his room, and the bride her chamber.' The unprecedented totality of the assembly: 'children, even nursing infants' (olelim veyone'key shadayim) — even those too young to participate consciously are included in the community's posture of repentance. 'The bridegroom leave his room' — in normal circumstances the new bridegroom was exempt from military duty for one year (Deut 24:5) and presumably from other communal obligations; the crisis suspends even the exemption for the newly married. The bridal chamber represents the innermost private sphere; even that privacy is broken open by this emergency.
2.17: 'Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests, the ministers of the LORD, weep and say, Spare your people, O LORD, and make not your heritage a reproach, a byword among the nations. Why should they say among the peoples, Where is their God?' The specific liturgical location: between the porch (ulam) and the altar (mizbei'ach) — this is the inner court of the Jerusalem temple, the space immediately before the altar of burnt offering. The prayer content invokes two covenant concerns: (1) YHWH's name/reputation among the nations ('Where is their God?' was the taunt of the nations when Israel suffered, cf. Ps 79:10, 115:2); (2) the status of Israel as YHWH's 'heritage' (nachalah), the land and people given as inheritance.
2.18: 'Then the LORD became jealous for his land and had pity on his people.' The pivot verse — divine response to the prayer of vv15-17. YHWH's 'jealousy' (yiqana) for his land is covenantal: the same 'jealousy' that drove him to judgment (2:18) is now turned toward restoration. 'Pity' (yachmol) — the root chamal means to spare through compassion. The land itself is the object of divine jealousy, not merely the people — consistent with the Deuteronomic covenant in which the land participates in the relationship between YHWH and Israel.
2.19: 'The LORD answered and said to his people, Behold, I am sending to you grain, wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied; and I will no more make you a reproach among the nations.' The divine answer is precisely the agricultural reversal of ch1: grain, wine, and oil — the three staple crops destroyed — are restored. 'I will no more make you a reproach' (velo-etten etchem od cherpah vagoyim) — the reproach of the nations (the 'where is their God?' taunt of v17) is answered by visible blessing. The covenant pattern: judgment brings reproach; restoration brings honor.
2.20: 'I will remove the northerner far from you, and drive him into a parched and desolate land, his vanguard into the eastern sea, and his rear guard into the western sea; its stench and foul smell will rise, for he has done great things.' The 'northerner' (hatzefoni) is the locust army — typically locusts come from the north in the Palestinian context, or the term may reflect ANE tradition of disaster coming from the north (cf. Jer 1:14, 4:6, 6:1 — 'out of the north evil comes'). 'Eastern sea' = the Dead Sea; 'western sea' = the Mediterranean. The locust horde driven into both seas and rotting — historically, when locust swarms reach the sea they drown and wash up in vast putrefying masses.
2.21: 'Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for the LORD has done great things!' The reversal call: the land personified (as it mourned in 1:10) is now called to rejoice (gili vesimchi). 'The LORD has done great things' (higdil YHWH la'asot) — the phrase echoes Ps 126:3 ('The LORD has done great things for us; we are glad'). The direct address to the land reflects the OT's view that the covenant affects the non-human creation — the land mourns under judgment and rejoices under blessing (cf. Ps 96:11-13, 98:7-9).
2.22: 'Fear not, you beasts of the field, for the pastures of the wilderness are green; the tree bears its fruit; the fig tree and vine give their full yield.' The beasts of the field — who groaned in 1:18 and panted in 1:20 — are addressed directly. The restoration is complete: pastures green, fig and vine yielding fully. The sequence from ch1 to ch2:22 forms a chiastic structure: (1) locust destroys vine and fig (1:7), (2) trees wither (1:12), (3) beasts groan (1:18), (4) animals pant for water (1:20); now reversed: (4) animals comforted (2:22), (3) pastures green (2:22), (2) fig and vine yield (2:22), (1) the full agricultural restoration follows in vv23-24.
2.23: 'Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in the LORD your God, for he has given the early rain for your vindication; he has poured down for you abundant rain, the early and the latter rain, as before.' The 'early rain' (moreh litzedaqah) — rain of righteousness/vindication — is the autumn rain that softens the soil for plowing (October-November in Palestine). The 'latter rain' (malqosh) is the spring rain (March-April) that fills out the grain before harvest. The two rainy seasons defined Palestinian agriculture; their regularity was a sign of covenant blessing (Deut 11:14). The rain as 'for your vindication' (litzedaqah) suggests that the restoration of rain demonstrates YHWH's righteousness in restoring his people.
2.24: 'The threshing floors shall be full of grain; the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.' The threshing floor (goren) — the paved hilltop platform where grain was threshed by oxen or a sledge — full of grain again, the vats flowing with wine and oil. This is the exact reversal of 1:17 (storehouses desolate, granaries torn down). The fullness of the threshing floor and overflowing vats are the material expression of covenant blessing — the land is productive again, the covenant relationship restored.
2.25: 'I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten, the hopper, the destroyer, and the cutter, my great army, which I sent among you.' YHWH identifies the locusts explicitly as 'my great army, which I sent among you' (cheyeli haggadol asher shillachti bachem). The locust plague was not a natural disaster in Joel's theology but a divine instrument. The promise to 'restore the years' (shillamti lachem et-hashenim) is remarkable: not merely to provide a good harvest next year, but to compensate for all that was lost. The four locust terms from 1:4 reappear in reverse order, emphasizing that this is a complete reversal.
2.26: 'You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the LORD your God, who has dealt wondrously with you. And my people shall never again be put to shame.' The covenant pattern of eating, satisfaction, and praise mirrors Deut 8:10-11: 'when you have eaten and are satisfied, praise the LORD your God.' The key prohibition in Deut 8:11 is 'do not forget' — here the promise is positive: eating leads to praising the name of YHWH. 'Never again be put to shame' (velo-yevoshu ammi le'olam) — the reproach that Israel suffered (v17, 19) is permanently removed. This promise becomes the background for Paul's 'whoever believes in him will not be put to shame' (Rom 10:11, quoting Isa 28:16).
2.27: 'You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am the LORD your God and there is none else. And my people shall never again be put to shame.' The covenant knowledge formula — 'you shall know that I am the LORD' (yeda'tem ki qirbi Yisrael) — combined with the monotheistic confession 'there is none else' (ve'ein od). This is the Deutero-Isaiah language (Isa 44:6, 45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22) applied to Joel's restoration. YHWH's presence 'in the midst of Israel' (bequirbi Yisrael) is the covenant's central promise — the divine presence in the Tabernacle/Temple that defines Israel as a covenant people.
2.28: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.' The great eschatological prophecy: ve'acharei-ken (after this/afterward) marks the transition from historical restoration to eschatological fulfillment. 'Pour out my Spirit on all flesh' (ashpoch et-ruchi al-kol-basar) — 'all flesh' (kol-basar) breaks all previous limits on prophetic experience: gender (sons AND daughters), age (old AND young), and social status (v29: servants AND handmaids). This democratization of the Spirit is the most radical promise in Joel and one of the most radical in the OT. Peter quotes it verbatim in Acts 2:17-21 as the interpretation of Pentecost.
2.29: 'Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.' The explicit inclusion of servants (avadim) and female servants (shefachot) is remarkable: these were the lowest social class in ancient Israel, with minimal legal standing. That the Spirit would be poured on them equally with the sons of the house radical. The legal category of slavery is not abolished here but spiritually transcended: all flesh, regardless of social standing, receives the Spirit equally. This egalitarian dimension of Joel's prophecy becomes central to Paul's theology ('there is neither slave nor free... in Christ Jesus,' Gal 3:28).
2.30: 'And I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth, blood and fire and columns of smoke.' The cosmic signs accompanying the Day of YHWH: blood (dam), fire (esh), and columns of smoke (timrot ashan). 'Columns of smoke' — the Hebrew timarot is related to the palm-tree (tamar) shape of a vertical smoke column. These signs have both immediate historical reference (volcanic or military events) and eschatological dimension. Peter quotes vv30-31 in Acts 2:19-20 as being fulfilled at Pentecost, understanding the cosmic signs as pointing to the significance of the Christ-event.
2.31: 'The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.' The astronomical portents: sun to darkness, moon to blood. 'Moon to blood' (hayareach ledam) — a lunar eclipse turns the moon copper-red; such eclipses were interpreted as omens in the ancient world. In the NT, this verse is quoted in Acts 2:20 (applied to Pentecost), Rev 6:12 (the sixth seal), and alluded to in Mark 13:24-25/Matt 24:29. The 'great and awesome day of the LORD' (yom YHWH haggadol vehanora) is the climactic event toward which all of Joel points — the final intervention of YHWH in history.
2.32: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved. For in Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there shall be those who escape, as the LORD has said, and among the survivors shall be those whom the LORD calls.' 'Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved' (vehayah kol asher-yiqra beshem YHWH yimalet) — the universal promise of salvation through the divine name. Paul quotes this in Rom 10:13 in the context of salvation through confessing Jesus as Lord (connecting with the divine-name theme in Phil 2:9-11). Acts 2:21 quotes it as fulfilled at Pentecost. The 'survivors' (serida) and those whom 'the LORD calls' introduce the election motif: both calling on the name and being called by YHWH are present.

3.1: 'For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem.' The phrase 'restore the fortunes' (shavti et-shevut) is a technical covenant-restoration term (cf. Jer 29:14, 30:3, Deut 30:3). The phrase literally means 'to turn the captivity' — the great reversal of exile through divine action. The temporal marker 'in those days and at that time' (bayamim hahem uvaaet hahiy) links this to the Day of YHWH events of ch2:28-32. The restoration of Judah and Jerusalem is the precondition for the judgment of the nations in vv2-16.
3.2: 'I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat. And I will enter into judgment with them there, on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel, because they have scattered them among the nations and have divided up my land.' The Valley of Jehoshaphat (ge Yoshafat) means 'YHWH judges' — whether this is an actual valley (traditionally identified with the Kidron Valley between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives) or a symbolic name is debated. The Kidron Valley association became strong in Jewish and Christian tradition; the Islamic tradition also places the judgment there. The charges against the nations: scattering (pizru) Israel among the nations and dividing (chaleku) YHWH's land. These are political and territorial crimes against both the people and the covenant land.
3.3: 'And have cast lots for my people, and have traded a boy for a prostitute, and have sold a girl for wine and have drunk it.' The slave trade described here — children sold for wine and sexual services — reflects the practice of enslaving captured populations and selling individuals to traders. The 'casting lots' (yadu goral) for people was the standard distribution of war captives among victorious soldiers (cf. Nah 3:10, where Nineveh's children are treated the same way). Selling a boy for the price of a prostitute, a girl for a cup of wine — the devaluation of human beings to the price of pleasure goods is the specific charge against the nations.
3.4: 'What are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia? Are you paying me back for something? If you are paying me back, I will return your payment on your own head swiftly and speedily.' Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia are the coastal neighbors who specifically profited from the slave trade: Phoenician (Tyrian) merchants controlled the Mediterranean slave trade, while Philistines had long been Israel's land-based enemy. Amos 1:6, 9 makes identical charges: Amos condemns Gaza (Philistia) for selling whole populations to Edom, and Tyre for delivering entire communities to Edom. The rhetorical question 'what are you to me?' (mah-attem li) challenges them to justify their actions.
3.5: 'Because you have taken my silver and my gold, and have carried my rich treasures into your temples.' 'My silver and my gold' — YHWH owns the treasures of Israel, and their removal to foreign temples (cf. 1 Kings 14:26: Shishak of Egypt carrying off the temple treasury) is theft from YHWH himself. The specific destination — 'your temples' (heikhaloteihem) — means the Phoenician and Philistine cult temples received the consecrated objects of YHWH's sanctuary. This is both robbery and sacrilege: pagan temples housing the holy vessels of YHWH is an offense to the divine presence.
3.6: 'You have sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks, so as to remove them far from their own border.' The Greeks here are the Ionians (Yavanim) — the term for Greeks in the OT (Gen 10:2, Isa 66:19, Ezek 27:13). The Phoenician cities of Tyre and Sidon were active in the Greek slave trade in the Eastern Mediterranean. The selling of Judean captives 'far from their border' — into the Greek world where return would be nearly impossible — is the specific geographical dimension of the punishment: they removed Israel from its covenant land, so YHWH will use Israel to remove them from their power base.
3.7: 'Behold, I will stir them up from the place to which you have sold them, and I will return your payment on your own head.' The divine reversal: YHWH himself will 'stir up' (me'ir) the sold slaves from wherever they have been scattered. This becomes the basis for the eschatological ingathering theme — the Diaspora Jews scattered among the nations will be gathered back. The retaliation formula 'return your payment on your own head' (hashivoti gemulchem bero'shechem) is the lex talionis applied at the national level: what they did to Israel will be done to them.
3.8: 'I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hand of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans, to a nation far away, for the LORD has spoken.' The Sabeans (Sheba) were the people of modern Yemen (Arabia Felix) — famous in the ancient world for their trading networks (1 Kings 10:1-13, the Queen of Sheba). The selling of Tyrian and Philistine children to the Sabeans mirrors exactly what they did to Judean children: long-distance slave trade with no return. 'For the LORD has spoken' (ki YHWH diber) — the prophetic authorization formula concludes the oracle.
3.9: 'Proclaim this among the nations: Consecrate for war; stir up the mighty men. Let all the men of war draw near; let them come up.' The ironic summons to the nations: come to the valley to fight. This is the opposite of the Isaianic vision (Isa 2:4, Mic 4:3: 'beat swords into plowshares'); here it is 'beat plowshares into swords' (v10). The nations are being summoned not to be blessed but to be judged — YHWH as the divine warrior assembles his enemies precisely so he can defeat them comprehensively. The 'consecrate for war' (qadshu milchamah) uses the ritual preparation language for holy war.
3.10: 'Beat your plowshares into swords, and your pruning hooks into spears; let the weak say, I am a warrior.' The deliberate reversal of Isa 2:4 and Mic 4:3 ('they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks') is rhetorically pointed: those peacetime prophecies envisioned an eschatological age of peace; Joel's summons inverts that image to call the nations to the final battle where they will be destroyed. 'Let the weak say, I am a warrior' (yomar challash gibbor ani) — even the weakest nations should come, because no human strength will matter in this judgment; YHWH will defeat them all equally.
3.11: 'Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather yourselves there. Bring down your warriors, O LORD.' The gathering of the nations to the valley — and then the petition to YHWH: 'bring down your warriors (gibborim), O LORD' — meaning the heavenly host that YHWH will deploy against the assembled nations. This inverts the nations' expectation: they come as warriors but encounter YHWH's heavenly warriors. The divine army concept (the host of heaven as YHWH's military force) appears in Josh 5:14-15 (commander of YHWH's army at Jericho), 2 Kings 6:17 (horses and chariots of fire), and Rev 19:14 (the armies of heaven).
3.12: 'Let the nations stir themselves up and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.' YHWH sitting to judge (eshevu lishpot) in the Valley of Jehoshaphat — the image of the divine judge enthroned in the valley between the nations. The 'surrounding nations' (goyim missaviv) — all nations that encircle and threaten Israel. The ancient Near Eastern concept of divine judgment involved the deity enthroned with the assembly of divine beings and human representatives; Joel draws on this imagery for the eschatological court.
3.13: 'Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in, tread, for the winepress is full. The vats overflow, for their evil is great.' The harvest and winepress as images of divine judgment — the nations are the crop ripe for harvest, the grape cluster pressed in the winepress of wrath. This imagery is developed extensively in Rev 14:14-20 (the harvest of the earth and the winepress of God's wrath, which 'was trodden outside the city, and blood flowed from the winepress, as high as a horse's bridle'). 'Their evil is great' (ki rabbah ra'atam) — the evil has reached the full measure that triggers divine judgment (cf. Gen 15:16: 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete').
3.14: 'Multitudes, multitudes, in the valley of decision! For the day of the LORD is near in the valley of decision.' The 'valley of decision' (ge hacharuts) — charuts means a sharp instrument (threshing sledge), or decisiveness/determination. The repeated 'multitudes, multitudes' (hamonim hamonim) is an emphatic double — the assembly of all nations in this valley for the decisive moment of divine judgment. The entire book has been building to this: the locust plague (ch1) → the Day of YHWH approaching (2:1-11) → the call to repentance (2:12-17) → the Spirit outpouring (2:28-32) → the gathering of nations to judgment (ch3).
3.15: 'The sun and the moon are darkened, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The cosmic signs from 2:10 and 2:31 recur — the final Day of YHWH is accompanied by the darkening of all heavenly lights. This creates an inclusio with the cosmic signs of ch2: the same phenomena that accompanied the locust-army now accompany the final judgment. The darkening of the cosmos represents the absolute sovereignty of the divine event: all natural light-sources yield to the theophanic presence. In the NT, the darkness at the crucifixion (Matt 27:45) and the eschatological darkness of the Apocalypse (Rev 6:12) draw on this tradition.
3.16: 'The LORD roars from Zion, and utters his voice from Jerusalem, and the heavens and the earth quake. But the LORD is a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the people of Israel.' The divine roar from Zion (YHWH mizion yish'ag) parallels Amos 1:2 exactly: both books use the same formula. The shaking heavens and earth recall 2:10. But the pivot: the very cosmic event that terrifies the nations is the refuge of YHWH's people. The 'stronghold' (maoz) image — YHWH as fortress of Israel — is the foundation of Ps 46 ('God is our refuge and strength... therefore we will not fear though the earth gives way'). Divine wrath and divine refuge are simultaneously expressed in the same event.
3.17: 'So you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain. And Jerusalem shall be holy, and strangers shall never again pass through it.' The covenant knowledge formula — 'you shall know that I am the LORD your God' — echoes 2:27 and completes the epistemological arc of the book. YHWH 'dwelling in Zion' (shochen betzion har qodshi) — the Zion theology of the Psalms (Pss 46, 48, 76): Jerusalem as YHWH's dwelling place, inviolable by enemies. 'Strangers shall never again pass through' (zarim lo-ya'avru vah od) — the final security of the holy city, end of the era of conquest and desecration.
3.18: 'And in that day the mountains shall drip sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk, and all the stream beds of Judah shall flow with water; and a fountain shall come forth from the house of the LORD and water the Valley of Shittim.' The eschatological abundance: mountains dripping wine, hills flowing milk, riverbeds full of water. These were proverbially used in descriptions of Canaan's fertility (Exod 3:8, 'flowing with milk and honey'). 'A fountain from the house of the LORD' (uma'yan mibbeit YHWH yetze) — a life-giving spring from the temple itself: this becomes the great eschatological image of Ezek 47:1-12 (the river from the temple) and Rev 22:1-2 (the river of the water of life from the throne of God). The Valley of Shittim (modern Jordan Valley) — notoriously dry — will be watered.
3.19: 'Egypt shall become a desolation and Edom a desolate wilderness, for the violence done to the people of Judah, because they have shed innocent blood in their land.' Egypt and Edom are the classic enemies of Judah — Egypt as the ancient oppressor (the Exodus) and Edom as the perpetual rival (descended from Esau, who sold his birthright; the Edomites celebrated when Jerusalem fell, Ps 137:7, Obad 10-14). 'Innocent blood' (dam-naki) — the specific charge of blood-guilt that cries to YHWH (cf. Gen 4:10, Abel's blood). Their desolation is explicitly punitive — not arbitrary but for specific covenant crimes against YHWH's people.
3.20: 'But Judah shall be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem to all generations.' The eternal habitation of Judah and Jerusalem — the covenantal promise of permanent settlement. Against the desolation of Egypt and Edom (v19), Judah and Jerusalem will be permanently inhabited. This is the Zion theology's ultimate expression: the eternal city, the inviolable dwelling of YHWH's people. In the NT, this finds its eschatological form in the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:2-3: 'I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven... and I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man').
3.21: 'I will avenge their blood, blood I have not avenged, for the LORD dwells in Zion.' The closing verse takes up the blood-guilt theme from v19: YHWH declares he will avenge 'blood I have not avenged' (venikkeiti damam lo-nikkeiti) — the long-deferred justice finally executed. 'For the LORD dwells in Zion' (vaYHWH shochen betzion) — the book ends where it is most rooted: YHWH's presence in Zion is both the guarantee of Israel's protection and the basis of his ultimate judicial authority. The dwelling of YHWH in Zion is the eschatological foundation that makes all the promises possible.
